items.q: What is the difference between vacuum forming and injection moulding?
items.correct: Vacuum forming shapes a heated thermoplastic sheet over a mould using suction; injection moulding injects molten plastic into a closed mould under high pressure.
items.misconception: They are interchangeable names for the same plastic process.
items.random_wrong: Vacuum forming is used for metals and injection moulding for wood.
items.why: Both involve plastics and moulds, so they blur together. Sheet plus suction gives thin shells (packaging, trays); molten plastic plus pressure gives solid detailed parts.

items.q: Is mass production the best choice for every product?
items.correct: No - mass production only pays off at high volumes; for small quantities or custom products, the enormous tooling and set-up costs make batch or one-off production more economical.
items.misconception: Yes - mass production always gives the lowest total cost.
items.random_wrong: Yes - because mass-produced goods are always higher quality.
items.why: Students see low unit cost and generalise. The set-up investment must be spread over many units; below the break-even volume, simpler scales of production win.

items.q: Are CNC machining and 3D printing the same thing?
items.correct: No - CNC machining is subtractive, cutting material away from a block, while 3D printing is additive, building the part up layer by layer.
items.misconception: Yes - both are computer controlled, so they are the same process.
items.random_wrong: Yes - CNC is simply the older name for 3D printing.
items.why: Computer control is shared, but the material logic is opposite. Subtractive starts big and removes; additive starts empty and deposits, producing far less waste.

items.q: Injection moulding is most appropriate when a product requires:
items.correct: Very large numbers of identical plastic parts
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Very large numbers of identical plastic parts. “One unique handmade item” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: CNC machining is classed as which type of process?
items.correct: Subtractive - it cuts material away under computer control
items.why: Correct answer: Subtractive - it cuts material away under computer control. “Additive” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Vacuum forming requires the material to be:
items.correct: A thermoplastic sheet that softens when heated
items.misconception: A thermosetting resin that has fully cured
items.why: The answer examiners want is: A thermoplastic sheet that softens when heated. “A thermosetting resin that has fully cured” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A bakery making 200 loaves of one bread type, then switching the same equipment to make 150 rolls, is using:
items.why: It's Batch production. “Continuous production” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A workshop produces a batch of 500 components at a rate of 25 per hour. How many hours does the batch take?
items.why: Exact answer: 20 hours. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
